questions.question: Explain how the discriminant helps in determining the nature of roots of a quadratic equation.
questions.answer: The discriminant is b² − 4ac. If it is positive, the roots are real and distinct; if zero, the roots are real and equal; if negative, the roots are not real.

questions.question: Explain the factorisation method of solving quadratic equations with its limitations.
questions.answer: In the factorisation method, the quadratic equation is expressed as a product of two linear factors. It is limited because not all quadratic equations can be easily factorised.

questions.question: Explain the completing the square method and when it is preferred.
questions.answer: Completing the square converts the quadratic equation into a perfect square. It is preferred when factorisation is difficult.

questions.question: Explain why the quadratic formula can solve all quadratic equations.
questions.answer: The quadratic formula is derived from completing the square and works for all values of a, b and c where a ≠ 0.

questions.question: Explain the graphical method of solving quadratic equations.
questions.answer: In the graphical method, the roots are obtained as x-coordinates where the parabola intersects the x-axis.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between roots of a quadratic equation and its graph.
questions.answer: The roots correspond to the x-intercepts of the parabola representing the quadratic equation.

questions.question: Explain why a quadratic equation can have at most two real roots.
questions.answer: Because its degree is two and the parabola can intersect the x-axis at most twice.

questions.question: Explain how the value of coefficient a affects the shape of the parabola.
questions.answer: The value of a determines whether the parabola opens upward or downward and how wide or narrow it is.

questions.question: Explain the meaning of real roots and imaginary roots.
questions.answer: Real roots are actual numerical solutions, while imaginary roots occur when the discriminant is negative.

questions.question: Explain why quadratic equations are used in projectile motion problems.
questions.answer: Projectile motion follows a parabolic path, which is represented by quadratic equations.

questions.question: Explain how quadratic equations are used in finding maximum and minimum values.
questions.answer: The vertex of the parabola gives the maximum or minimum value depending on the direction of opening.

questions.question: Explain why completing the square helps in finding the vertex of a parabola.
questions.answer: Completing the square rewrites the equation in vertex form, making the vertex easy to identify.

questions.question: Explain why discriminant is important in real-life applications.
questions.answer: It helps determine whether real solutions exist for practical problems.

questions.question: Explain the advantages of quadratic formula over factorisation.
questions.answer: The quadratic formula works for all quadratic equations, unlike factorisation.

questions.question: Explain why graphical method is not always preferred.
questions.answer: Because it gives approximate solutions and depends on accuracy of graph drawing.

questions.question: Explain the connection between quadratic equations and area problems.
questions.answer: Many area problems lead to equations involving squared terms, resulting in quadratic equations.

questions.question: Explain why quadratic equations are polynomial equations.
questions.answer: Because they involve variables raised only to non-negative integer powers.

questions.question: Explain the significance of the vertex in real-life problems.
questions.answer: The vertex gives maximum height, minimum cost, or optimal value in real-life situations.

questions.question: Explain why roots may not always be integers.
questions.answer: Because the discriminant may not be a perfect square.

questions.question: Explain how quadratic equations are used in engineering.
questions.answer: They are used to model structures, trajectories, and optimisation problems.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between coefficient b and axis of symmetry.
questions.answer: The axis of symmetry is given by x = −b / 2a.

questions.question: Explain why quadratic equations are important in physics.
questions.answer: They describe motion under gravity and other physical phenomena.

questions.question: Explain the use of quadratic equations in economics.
questions.answer: They are used to find maximum profit and minimum cost.

questions.question: Explain why a quadratic equation may not have real roots.
questions.answer: Because the discriminant may be negative.

questions.question: Explain the educational importance of quadratic equations.
questions.answer: They build the foundation for higher mathematics and problem-solving skills.
